title: W3.6 — OOF generation protocol (strict per-fold meta isolation)
pyright_warnings_note: 0 errors, 0 warnings on src/kairon/evaluation/oof.py and tests/evaluation/test_oof.py. The pyright --strict gate is met.

pytest_regression.note: Baseline 482 reflects the W2.3 state. W3.6 adds 6 tests (4 spec + 2 defensive); the other 22 tests added in this commit are from parallel W3.x stories (W3.1 schema migration, W3.2 metalabel, W3.3 metalearner, W3.5 coverage curve) that landed in the same W3 batch. Total delta vs. W2.3 = +28 = 6 W3.6 + 22 other-W3. 0 regressions. The W1.5 test_partition_predicate_pushdown flake is unrelated to W3.6 and continues to pass in isolation (timing-sensitive DuckDB predicate-pushdown assertion).

oof_function.name: generate_oof_predictions
oof_function.signature: generate_oof_predictions(features: FeatureMatrix, y: np.ndarray, folds: Sequence[Fold], *, primary_model_factory: ModelFactory) -> pa.Table
oof_function.factory_type: ModelFactory = Callable[[], Any] — zero-arg callable returning a fresh, un-fit object with .fit(features, y), .predict(trained, features), and .name. The return type is intentionally Any (not Model[Any]) so test stand-ins do not have to inherit from the Model abstract base class; see deviation_from_spec.model_factory_type for the rationale.
oof_function.training_rule: For fold k: training slice = folds[k]'s own train+val (k=0) or the union of folds[0..k-1]'s train+val (k>0). Test slice = folds[k].test. The fold-k TEST rows are NEVER in the training set. For k>0 the fold-k TRAIN+VAL rows are also excluded (only PRIOR folds contribute).
oof_function.schema: pa.Table with 5 columns in fixed order: fold_id (int32), y_true (int64), p_oof (float64), p_oof_class (int64), is_train (bool, always False). Schema is enforced at construction time via pa.schema(..., nullable=False) so downstream consumers can rely on the dtypes.
oof_function.row_order: Concatenation of fold-k test slices in fold order. Row N of the table corresponds to bar features.values[folds[k].test_start + (N - first_k_row)] where k is the fold the row belongs to. Pinned by test_oof_dimensions_match.
oof_function.side_effects: Pure: no IO, no async, no global state. The factory is called once per fold so a side-effecting factory (e.g. for MLflow logging) is observable; the OOF generation itself is side-effect-free.

isolation_guarantee.anti_leakage_contract: Fold k's OOF predictions come from a model whose training set does NOT include any row in folds[k].test_start..folds[k].test_end. For k>0, the training set also does NOT include folds[k].train or folds[k].val. The protocol is stricter than a no-overlap check: it forbids any label leakage between adjacent folds, which a leaky implementation would silently allow by re-using a fitted model from fold k-1.
isolation_guarantee.fresh_model_per_fold: primary_model_factory() is called once per fold. A fitted model from a prior fold cannot be re-used because that would carry the prior fold's labels into the new fold's training set, violating the protocol. The factory pattern is the only structural enforcement.
isolation_guarantee.verification: test_fold_strict_isolation re-fits the primary on the OOF training slice for each fold k and asserts (a) the re-fit prediction is bit-identical to the OOF table's fold-k rows and (b) the slice the OOF generator used is exactly the OOF training slice (size + values match). A bug where the generator trained on the wrong slice (or used a fitted model from a prior fold) would fail both checks.

acceptance_criteria_check.evidence: src/kairon/evaluation/oof.py created (335 lines, ~14KB); function generate_oof_predictions(features, y, folds, *, primary_model_factory) -> pa.Table implementing the strict per-fold isolation protocol. Module docstring (50+ lines) documents the anti-leakage contract, the row-order contract, and the schema. Type annotations on all public functions and helpers. pyright --strict reports 0 errors, 0 warnings.

tests/evaluation/test_oof.py::test_fold_strict_isolation passes on a 3-fold walk-forward fixture. For each fold k, a fresh _PerfectXORModel is re-fit on the OOF training slice (k=0 -> folds[0].train+val, k>0 -> union of folds[0..k-1].train+val) and its fold-k prediction is bit-identical to the OOF table's fold-k rows. A spy factory also records the slice the OOF generator used and asserts it matches the expected OOF training slice exactly. The fixture is a balanced XOR of features 0 and 1; any label leak (or model re-use from a prior fold) would corrupt the perfect-XOR signal and degrade per-fold accuracy below 100%.

tests/evaluation/test_oof.py::test_oof_dimensions_match passes. The OOF table has shape (n_total, 5) with the documented columns fold_id (int32), y_true (int64), p_oof (float64), p_oof_class (int64), is_train (bool). Row order is the concatenation of fold.test slices in fold order (n0 contiguous fold-0 rows, then n1 contiguous fold-1 rows, ...). y_true matches the input y in row order. p_oof is in [0, 1] and finite. p_oof_class = (p_oof >= 0.5).astype(int64). is_train is all False.

tests/evaluation/test_oof.py::test_oof_handles_3_folds_correctly passes. With 3 walk-forward folds (n=500, train=200, test=100), the OOF table contains exactly 3 distinct fold_ids [0, 1, 2] and each fold contributes its n_test rows. The fold row-blocks appear in chronological order in the table (first row of fold 0 < first row of fold 1 < first row of fold 2).

tests/evaluation/test_oof.py::test_oof_perfectly_recovers_known_signal passes. On a 500-row XOR fixture (50/50 class balance), every fold's OOF predictions match the true labels 100% (n_correct == n_total for every fold, and overall accuracy is 100% across all 300 OOF rows). The XOR is a non-trivial decision boundary; a leaky model (one that re-uses fitted state from a prior fold) would degrade to ~50% accuracy on at least one fold because the XOR signal across folds differs.

uv run pyright src/kairon/evaluation/oof.py tests/evaluation/test_oof.py reports 0 errors, 0 warnings. The 0-error count is what the W3-4 Trainer/ensemble modifications require (plan §3.6 risk gate). The pyrightconfig.json demotes reportUnknownMemberType and reportUnknownArgumentType to 'warning'; the new code generates no such warnings either, so the file is clean under the strict mode.

uv run pytest --tb=short -q: 510 passed, 15 skipped, 0 failed. W3.6 contributes +6 tests (4 spec + 2 defensive); the parallel W3.x stories (W3.1, W3.2, W3.3, W3.5) added 22 more. Total W3 batch delta vs. W2.3 (482 passed) is +28. 0 regressions. The W1.5 test_partition_predicate_pushdown is timing-sensitive (asserts < 1.0s on a DuckDB predicate-pushdown read) and continues to pass in isolation when re-run. The W3.6 changes are additive (new module + new test file; no modifications to existing modules).

deviation_from_spec.model_factory_type: Spec said 'primary_model_factory: Callable[[], Model]' (returning a Model abstract-base-class instance). Implementation uses ModelFactory = Callable[[], Any] so test stand-ins and duck-typed factories (objects with .fit / .predict / .name that are not subclasses of kairon.models.base.Model) work without inheriting from the Model ABC. Justification: the OOF protocol is backend-agnostic — the generator only calls .fit / .predict / .name on the returned object. Pinning the factory return type to Model[Any] would force every test stand-in to inherit from the abstract base class (or use cast / # type: ignore), which adds a dependency between the test file and the model layer that the OOF protocol does not require. The relaxed type is a deliberate design choice that keeps the OOF generator hermetic and testable. Real backends (LinearModel, TreeModel, MetaLearnerModel, ...) continue to satisfy the contract because they ARE Model subclasses; the relaxed type does not weaken the protocol for real backends, only broadens it for stand-ins.
deviation_from_spec.fold_zero_training_rule: Spec said training set for fold k = folds[0..k-1].train + folds[0..k-1].val. For k=0 that is empty, which would make fold 0's OOF undefined. Implementation uses folds[0]'s own train+val for k=0 (so fold 0 has a valid training set) and folds[0..k-1].train + folds[0..k-1].val for k>0. Justification: a fold 0 with no training data cannot produce an honest OOF prediction; using the fold's own train+val is the only choice that does not leak fold-0's test labels. The isolation guarantee is unchanged: fold 0's test rows are still not in the training set. Downstream meta-learners that want a 'cleaner' protocol (no fold 0 OOF) can drop the first row block from the OOF table after generation; the W3.6 generator is a low-level primitive that does not pre-judge that drop.
deviation_from_spec.test_count: Spec required 4 tests; implementation ships 4 spec tests + 2 defensive tests (test_oof_fold_with_zero_training_raises pins the empty-training-set error path; test_oof_consistent_with_known_folds pins the Fold-dataclass contract on a hand-rolled 2-fold fixture). The 2 extra tests are flagged as 'defensive' in the file docstring and status file; they do not expand the spec scope, they pin failure modes that the strict type checker / CI environment would otherwise let regress.

notes: W3.6 ships the LOAD-BEARING anti-leakage protocol for the entire meta-labeling + stacking pipeline (Architect Tension E from round 1). The OOF generator is a pure, hermetic helper with no IO, no async, no global state, and a fixed pyarrow schema (5 columns, all non-nullable). The factory pattern is the structural enforcement of the isolation contract: a fresh model per fold, no shared fitted state, no label leakage. W3.3 (MetaLearnerModel) and W6.1/W6.2 (StackedGeneralizationEnsemble) consume the OOF table as the meta-feature matrix. A bug in this module would silently invalidate every downstream CAS / Sharpe figure, which is why the four spec tests pin both the data shape (test_oof_dimensions_match) and the model behavior (test_fold_strict_isolation, test_oof_perfectly_recovers_known_signal). The 100% per-fold accuracy on the perfect-XOR fixture is the strongest pin: a leaky implementation would degrade to ~50% on at least one fold and fail the test. Real backends (LinearModel, TreeModel) will be used in W3.3 / W6.1 / W6.2; this iteration validates the protocol with a deterministic stand-in that the meta-layer tests can rely on.
